pairs.question: Who was Elmer Charles 'Mike' Alft Jr. and what were his notable roles in Elgin, Illinois?
pairs.answer: Elmer Charles 'Mike' Alft Jr. (1925–2021) was an American historian and former mayor of Elgin, Illinois. He served as mayor from 1967 to 1971 after being elected in 1963 as a city councilman. Beyond politics, Alft dedicated over 40 years to teaching at Elgin High School and part-time instruction at Elgin Community College. He also held roles such as secretary of the Board of Trustees of the Gail Borden Public Library District and participated in various local government capacities.

pairs.question: What educational background did Elmer Alft have?
pairs.answer: Elmer Alft graduated Phi Beta Kappa from Grinnell College in 1949 with a bachelor's degree. He earned his master's degree from Syracuse University in 1950.

pairs.question: How did Elmer Alft contribute to Elgin's historical documentation?
pairs.answer: Alft was a prolific historian of Elgin, publishing several books on its history and writing hundreds of articles for the *Elgin Daily Courier-News*. He also authored a biweekly column focused on local history, leaving a lasting record of the city's past.

pairs.question: What was the significance of Alft's appearance on C-SPAN in 2001?
pairs.answer: In 2001, Alft appeared on C-SPAN as part of a panel of local historians discussing the works of Upton Sinclair and Theodore Dreiser, highlighting his reputation as a historian and his expertise in regional literature.

pairs.question: Explain the political career timeline of Elmer Alft in Elgin, Illinois.
pairs.answer: Alft began his political career in April 1963 when he was elected to the Elgin City Council in an at-large election. Four years later, in 1967, he was elected mayor, succeeding Clyde Shales. By custom, he did not seek reelection in 1971, and William Rauschenberger succeeded him as mayor.

pairs.question: What was the custom regarding mayoral reelection during Elmer Alft's tenure?
pairs.answer: During Alft's tenure as mayor (1967–1971), it was customary not to seek reelection after a four-year term. This tradition led Alft to step down in 1971, allowing William Rauschenberger to be elected as his successor.
